items.explanation: The opposite is true: serum potassium <3.3 mmol/L occurs in ~70% of patients with ectopic ACTH secretion but in <10% of patients with pituitary-dependent Cushing's disease, reflecting the more severe, abrupt hypercortisolism of ectopic sources.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Presentation and Diagnosis: "Serum potassium levels <3.3 mmol/L are evident in ~70% of patients with ectopic ACTH secretion but are seen in <10% of patients with pituitary-dependent Cushing's syndrome."

items.statement: Bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing's disease can precipitate Nelson's syndrome because removal of cortisol eliminates negative feedback on a residual corticotrope adenoma.
items.explanation: Adrenalectomy removes cortisol's negative feedback while leaving residual tumor tissue, which can then proliferate rapidly and secrete high levels of ACTH/MSH-related peptides, producing Nelson's syndrome (rapid tumor enlargement and hyperpigmentation).
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Adrenalectomy in the setting of residual corticotrope adenoma tissue predisposes to the development of Nelson's syndrome, a disorder characterized by rapid pituitary tumor enlargement and increased pigmentation secondary to high ACTH levels."

items.assertion: Pegvisomant therapy in acromegaly is monitored using serum IGF-1 levels rather than GH levels.
items.reason: Pegvisomant blocks the GH receptor peripherally and does not act on the pituitary tumor, so GH secretion is unaffected (and may even increase).
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: because pegvisomant does not lower pituitary GH output, GH cannot reflect drug efficacy — only IGF-1 (and clinical response) can.
items.reference: Acromegaly — GH Receptor Antagonist: "Pegvisomant antagonizes endogenous GH action by blocking peripheral GH binding to its receptor. Consequently, serum IGF-1 levels are suppressed... GH levels, however, remain elevated as the drug does not target the pituitary adenoma."

items.assertion: Dopamine agonist therapy can normalize serum prolactin in a patient with a nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma causing stalk-compression hyperprolactinemia.
items.reason: Normalization of prolactin with a dopamine agonist confirms that the underlying pituitary mass is a prolactinoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is true — dopamine agonists act on the normal lactotropes regardless of the cause of hyperprolactinemia. The reason is false: PRL normalization does NOT confirm a prolactinoma, since a nonfunctioning adenoma's mass does not shrink with dopamine agonist therapy.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Presentation and Diagnosis: "It is important to remember that hyperprolactinemia caused secondarily by the mass effects of nonlactotrope lesions is also corrected by treatment with dopamine agonists despite failure to shrink the underlying mass. Consequently, PRL suppression by dopamine agonists does not necessarily indicate that the underlying lesion is a prolactinoma."

items.assertion: Men with prolactinomas are typically diagnosed with larger tumors than women.
items.reason: The female-to-male ratio for microprolactinomas is approximately 20:1, while the ratio for macroadenomas is near 1:1.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason restates the epidemiologic consequence rather than explaining WHY men present later: the underlying explanation is that male hypogonadal symptoms (decreased libido, impotence) are less conspicuous and less often prompt early evaluation.
items.reference: Prolactinoma — Etiology and Prevalence: "Men tend to present with larger tumors than women, possibly because the features of male hypogonadism are less readily evident."

items.assertion: Bromocriptine, rather than cabergoline, is preferred for treating prolactinoma when a woman desires pregnancy.
items.reason: Bromocriptine has a longer half-life and higher D2-receptor affinity than cabergoline, resulting in prolonged fetal drug exposure.
items.explanation: The assertion is true. The reason is false and has the pharmacology backwards: it is CABERGOLINE that is long-acting with high D2-receptor affinity, which is why it is avoided when fertility is desired; bromocriptine is short-acting with extensive pregnancy safety data.
items.reference: Pregnancy: "Because cabergoline is long-acting with a high D2-receptor affinity, it is not recommended for use in women when fertility is desired."

items.assertion: A pituitary mass with elevated free α-subunit and an inappropriately normal TSH despite elevated free T4 suggests a TSH-secreting adenoma.
items.reason: Resistance to thyroid hormone (RTH) also presents with a pituitary mass and elevated free α-subunit, making it indistinguishable from a TSH-secreting adenoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is true. The reason is false: RTH is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by thyroid hormone receptor β mutations and does NOT produce a pituitary mass or elevated free α-subunit — these features instead favor a TSH-secreting adenoma.
items.reference: TSH-Secreting Adenomas: "It is important to exclude other causes of inappropriate TSH secretion, such as resistance to thyroid hormone, an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the thyroid hormone β receptor... The presence of a pituitary mass and elevated β subunit levels are suggestive of a TSH-secreting tumor."

items.assertion: Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling is performed routinely in nearly all patients with ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome before pituitary surgery.
items.reason: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling is a technically simple procedure with no associated complications.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. IPSS is reserved for cases where MRI does not clearly show an adenoma and the source remains uncertain — it is avoided when a clear adenoma is already visible. It is also technically demanding, with rare (~0.05%) neurovascular complications.
items.reference: Inferior Petrosal Venous Sampling: "An increased central:peripheral ACTH ratio (>2) before and a peak central:peripheral ACTH ratio >3 after CRH injection confirm the presence of a pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. The sensitivity of this test is >95%, with very rare false-positive results."

items.assertion: Pasireotide LAR therapy for Cushing's disease is associated with a high rate of new-onset hyperglycemia and diabetes.
items.reason: Pasireotide normalizes 24-hour urinary free cortisol in approximately 20% of patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion. The hyperglycemia results from pasireotide suppressing pancreatic insulin and incretin secretion via somatostatin receptors — an effect independent of its rate of cortisol normalization.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Notably, hyperglycemia and new-onset diabetes develop in up to 70% of patients, likely due to suppressed pancreatic secretion of insulin and incretins."

items.assertion: Mifepristone therapy for Cushing's syndrome is titrated using serial ACTH and cortisol levels.
items.reason: Mifepristone blocks the glucocorticoid receptor peripherally without lowering ACTH or cortisol secretion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — ACTH and cortisol remain elevated during mifepristone therapy and cannot serve as biomarkers, so clinical parameters (glycemic control, blood pressure) guide dosing instead. The reason is true and is precisely why the assertion is false.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Mifepristone... blocks peripheral cortisol action and is approved to treat hyperglycemia in Cushing's disease. Because the drug does not target the pituitary tumor, both ACTH and cortisol levels remain elevated, thus obviating a reliable circulating biomarker."

items.assertion: Patients on osilodrostat for Cushing's disease should be monitored for hypokalemia and hypertension.
items.reason: Osilodrostat inhibits 11β-hydroxylase, causing accumulation of upstream adrenal steroid precursors with mineralocorticoid activity.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: blocking 11β-hydroxylase causes precursor steroids to accumulate and act on mineralocorticoid receptors, producing hypokalemia and hypertension, while excessive cortisol blockade can cause adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "Patients should be closely monitored for development of hypocortisolism and adrenal insufficiency. Elevated adrenal hormone precursors may lead to hypokalemia and hypertension."

items.assertion: Development of symptomatic ACTH deficiency in the weeks after successful resection of a corticotrope adenoma is an unfavorable sign suggesting incomplete tumor removal.
items.reason: Chronic suppression of normal corticotrope cells by long-standing hypercortisolism requires time to recover after the tumor is removed.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — postoperative ACTH deficiency lasting up to 12 months is EXPECTED and actually a favorable sign of surgical cure, requiring temporary glucocorticoid replacement. The reason is true and is precisely why this deficiency occurs.
items.reference: Cushing's Disease — Treatment: "After successful tumor resection, most patients experience a postoperative period of symptomatic ACTH deficiency that may last up to 12 months. This usually requires low-dose cortisol replacement, as patients experience steroid withdrawal symptoms and have a suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis."

items.assertion: A small, asymptomatic nonfunctioning pituitary microadenoma with no threat to vision can be followed with serial MRI rather than immediate surgery.
items.reason: Macroadenomas causing compressive mass effects are treated with transsphenoidal surgery, which improves vision in about 70% of patients with preoperative visual field defects.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason describes management of a different scenario (symptomatic macroadenomas) rather than explaining the assertion. The actual reason microadenomas can be observed is that the probability of growth of a nonfunctioning microadenoma is very low.
items.reference: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas — Treatment: "As the probability of nonfunctioning microadenoma growth is very low, asymptomatic small nonfunctioning microadenomas with no threat to vision may be followed with an MRI after 3 years, with serial MRI and visual field testing thereafter as needed."

items.assertion: A recurrent papillary craniopharyngioma harboring a BRAF V600E mutation may respond to BRAF inhibitor therapy, with or without a MEK inhibitor.
items.reason: Activating BRAF V600E mutations are particularly associated with the papillary histologic variant of craniopharyngioma.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: because papillary craniopharyngiomas frequently carry this driver mutation, BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib or vemurafenib), alone or with MEK inhibitors (trametinib or cobimetinib), have produced long-term growth responses.
items.reference: Sellar Masses: "As some craniopharyngiomas (particularly papillary) are associated with activated BRAF V600E mutations, use of BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib or vemurafenib) either alone or in combination with MEK inhibitors (trametinib or cobimetinib) has resulted in long-term growth responses in some patients."

items.subtopic: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Headache Correlation with Intrasellar Masses
items.content: Headaches are common in small intrasellar tumors, but their severity does not correlate well with the size or extension of the adenoma.
keyPoints: Headaches can occur even without suprasellar extension due to confined nature and pressure changes within the sella.
Severity of headaches poorly correlates with adenoma size or extent.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)
items.title: Visual Loss Mechanisms in Suprasellar Extension
items.content: Suprasellar extension of pituitary tumors can lead to visual loss primarily through optic chiasm compression, with other mechanisms being rare.
keyPoints: Compression of the optic chiasm is the most common cause of visual loss due to suprasellar extension.
Rare causes include direct invasion of optic nerves or CSF flow obstruction.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)
items.title: Criteria for Monitoring and Resection of Pituitary Adenomas
items.content: Small intrasellar adenomas without hormone hypersecretion can be monitored with annual MRI, while larger macroadenomas may require resection due to potential invasiveness.
keyPoints: Incidental small lesions (incidentalomas) are monitored annually via MRI unless they grow.
Larger macroadenomas (>1 cm) should be considered for resection as about one-third can become invasive or cause pressure effects.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Pituitary Tumor Syndromes | EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.title: Initial Laboratory Evaluation for Suspected Pituitary Adenomas
items.content: When a pituitary adenoma is suspected based on MRI, initial hormonal evaluation should include specific tests to assess hormone levels and potential hypopituitarism.
keyPoints: Basal prolactin (PRL) measurement is essential for identifying prolactin-secreting tumors.
Insulin-like growth factor (IGF)-1 helps evaluate growth hormone excess, such as in acromegaly.
24-h urinary free cortisol (UFC) or overnight oral dexamethasone suppression test assesses Cushing's disease.
α subunit, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and luteinizing hormone (LH) levels are evaluated to rule out other pituitary disorders.
Thyroid function tests identify potential hypopituitarism affecting thyroid regulation.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 2) | OVERVIEW

items.title: Histologic Evaluation of Pituitary Tumors
items.content: Immunohistochemical staining during transsphenoidal surgery aids in confirming the clinical and laboratory diagnosis, especially for equivocal hormone studies or nonfunctioning tumors.
keyPoints: Immunohistochemical staining is used to confirm hormone production and cell-type specific transcription factors.
Provides a definitive histologic diagnosis when hormonal studies are inconclusive.
Crucial in cases of clinically nonfunctioning pituitary tumors.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · EVALUATION OF SELLAR MASSES (part 2) | OVERVIEW

items.title: Preferred Surgical Approach for Pituitary Tumors
items.content: Transsphenoidal resection is the preferred surgical approach for most pituitary tumors due to its minimally invasive nature, avoiding cranial invasion and manipulation of brain tissue. It allows for effective visualization and microdissection of small tumors not visible by MRI using intraoperative microscopy or endoscopic techniques with three-dimensional localization.
keyPoints: Transsphenoidal resection is the desired approach for most pituitary tumors.
Intraoperative microscopy aids in distinguishing adenomatous from normal tissue.
Endoscopic techniques improve visualization and access to tumor tissue.
Avoids cranial invasion compared to subfrontal approaches.
Surgical experience significantly impacts outcomes.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · TRANSSPHENOIDAL SURGERY

items.title: Indications and Risks of Transsphenoidal Surgery for Pituitary Tumors
items.content: Transsphenoidal surgery is indicated for pituitary tumors causing hormonal hypersecretion or mass effects such as headaches, visual field defects, cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus, or apoplexy. It aims to selectively excise the tumor while preserving normal pituitary tissue when possible. However, non-selective resection may be necessary in certain cases, increasing the risk of postoperative hypopituitarism.
keyPoints: Indicated for hormonal hypersecretion and mass effects like headaches or visual defects.
Surgical decompression needed for expanding masses with symptoms.
Selective excision preferred to preserve normal pituitary tissue.
Non-selective resection may lead to postoperative hypopituitarism.
Complications: ~1% mortality, 20% transient AVP-D and hypopituitarism, up to 10% permanent complications like cranial nerve damage or visual disturbances.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · TRANSSPHENOIDAL SURGERY

items.title: Radiation Therapy as an Adjunct in Pituitary Tumor Management
items.content: Radiation therapy is often used post-surgery for pituitary tumors to manage residual tumor tissue, particularly when medical therapy isn't effective. It's crucial for preventing growth or recurrence of nonfunctioning tumor tissues.
keyPoints: Used as primary or adjunctive treatment for pituitary/parasellar masses.
Typically reserved for postsurgical management due to slow onset.
Effective in ablating significant postoperative residual nonfunctioning tumor tissue.
PRL-, GH-, ACTH-, and TSH-secreting tumors may be managed with medical therapy instead.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · RADIATION

items.title: Long-term Effects and Risks of Radiation Therapy for Pituitary Tumors
items.content: Radiation therapy can lead to significant long-term effects, including hormone synthesis failure and secondary tumor development. Lifelong follow-up is essential due to the risk of pituitary hormone loss.
keyPoints: More than 50% develop loss of GH, ACTH, TSH, and/or gonadotropin secretion within 10 years.
Optic nerve damage occurs in ~2% of patients.
Cranial nerve damage is uncommon with doses <2 Gy per session and max <50 Gy total.
Cumulative risk of secondary tumor: 1.3% after 10 years, 1.9% after 20 years.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · RADIATION

items.title: Treatment of Pituitary Tumors
items.content: Pituitary tumors require specific medical therapies based on tumor type. Dopamine agonists are used for prolactinomas, while somatostatin receptor ligands and GH receptor antagonists treat acromegaly. ACTH-secreting tumors may respond to SRLs or adrenal-directed therapy. Nonfunctioning tumors typically necessitate surgery or irradiation.
keyPoints: Dopamine agonists for prolactinomas
SRLs and GH receptor antagonist for acromegaly
ACTH-secreting tumors may respond to SRLs
Nonfunctioning tumors require surgery/irradiation
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · MEDICAL | SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.title: Craniopharyngiomas: Presentation and Management
items.content: Craniopharyngiomas are benign, suprasellar masses presenting with headaches, visual deficits, and hypopituitarism. They often present before age 20 with increased intracranial pressure symptoms. MRI is preferred for evaluation; treatment involves surgical resection and possibly radiotherapy to improve survival.
keyPoints: Present with headaches, visual field deficits
~90% have hypopituitarism, ~10% AVP-D
MRI superior for evaluating cystic structure
Surgery followed by radiation improves 10-year survival to 70–90%
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · MEDICAL | SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.title: Rathke's Cysts: Diagnosis and Clinical Relevance
items.content: Rathke's cysts, found in ~20% of individuals at autopsy, are small cysts that may cause compressive symptoms like AVP-D and hyperprolactinemia due to stalk compression. They are diagnosed by visualizing the cyst wall on MRI.
keyPoints: ~20% prevalence at autopsy
May present with AVP-D and hyperprolactinemia
Diagnosed by MRI visualization of cyst wall
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · MEDICAL | SELLAR MASSES (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Pituitary Metastases
items.content: Pituitary metastases occur in approximately 3% of cancer patients, with the majority originating from breast cancer. These metastatic deposits are typically found in the posterior pituitary and can present as AVP-D (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion). Diagnosis is challenging due to similarities with aggressive pituitary adenomas on MRI, often necessitating histologic examination.
keyPoints: Pituitary metastases occur in ~3% of cancer patients.
~50% of pituitary metastases originate from breast cancer; ~25% of metastatic breast cancer patients have pituitary deposits.
AVP-D can be a presenting feature of lung, gastrointestinal, and other pituitary metastases.
Diagnosis may require histologic examination due to difficulty distinguishing from aggressive adenomas on MRI.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · SELLAR MASSES (part 2)

items.title: Managing Hypothalamic Hamartomas
items.content: Hypothalamic hamartomas can overexpress neuropeptides like GnRH, leading to precocious puberty. Medical management with long-acting GnRH analogues suppresses gonadotropin secretion and controls premature pubertal development. These tumors may also be associated with Pallister-Hall syndrome, characterized by craniofacial abnormalities and other systemic issues.
keyPoints: Hypothalamic hamartomas overexpress neuropeptides such as GnRH, GHRH, and CRH.
Children with GnRH-producing tumors present with precocious puberty, psychomotor delay, and laughing-associated seizures.
Long-acting GnRH analogues are effective in suppressing gonadotropin secretion and controlling premature pubertal development.
Pallister-Hall syndrome may be associated with hypothalamic hamartomas.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · SELLAR MASSES (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Detection of Pituitary Adenomas
items.content: Pituitary adenomas are the most common cause of pituitary hormone hypersecretion and hyposecretion syndromes in adults, accounting for approximately 15% of all intracranial neoplasms. They have a population prevalence of about 80 per 100,000 individuals. At autopsy, up to one-quarter of all pituitary glands contain an unsuspected microadenoma (<10 mm diameter). Imaging detects small clinically inapparent lesions in at least 10% of individuals.
keyPoints: Pituitary adenomas account for ~15% of intracranial neoplasms.
Population prevalence is ~80/100,000.
Up to one-quarter of pituitaries harbor microadenomas (<10 mm) at autopsy.
Imaging detects small lesions in ≥10% of individuals.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis and Characteristics of Hormonally Active Pituitary Adenomas
items.content: Hormonally active pituitary adenomas arise from specific anterior pituitary cell types, leading to hormone hypersecretion. These tumors exhibit autonomous hormone secretion with diminished feedback responsiveness. Notably, hormone production does not always correlate with tumor size; small adenomas can cause significant clinical effects while larger ones may be asymptomatic.
keyPoints: Arise from anterior pituitary cell types (e.g., lactotrope, somatotrope).
Autonomous secretion with diminished feedback responsiveness.
~1/3 of adenomas are clinically nonfunctioning.
Hormone production not always correlated with tumor size.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Genetic and Molecular Mechanisms in Pituitary Tumor Development
items.content: Most pituitary adenomas are monoclonal, often arising from somatic mutations that confer growth advantages. In acromegaly, ~35% of GH-secreting tumors have Gα mutations leading to elevated cyclic AMP and GH secretion. Growth factors like bFGF and EGFR signaling also promote tumor proliferation. Genetic syndromes such as MEN 1 (due to MENIN mutations) and Carney complex (PRKAR1A mutations) are associated with pituitary tumors.
keyPoints: Most adenomas are monoclonal, often due to somatic mutations.
~35% of GH-secreting tumors have Gα mutations.
bFGF and EGFR signaling promote proliferation.
MEN 1 involves MENIN gene mutations; Carney complex involves PRKAR1A mutations.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.subtopic: PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA
items.title: Common Causes and Clinical Presentation of Hyperprolactinemia
items.content: Hyperprolactinemia is primarily caused by prolactin-secreting pituitary adenomas, particularly when PRL levels exceed 200 µg/L. Other causes include drugs, hypothyroidism, renal failure, and physiological conditions like pregnancy. Clinically, hyperprolactinemia presents with amenorrhea and galactorrhea in women, and diminished libido and infertility in men.
keyPoints: Prolactinomas are the most common cause of PRL levels >200 µg/L.
Common causes include drugs (e.g., antipsychotics), hypothyroidism, renal failure, and physiological states like pregnancy.
Clinical presentation: Women experience amenorrhea, galactorrhea; men have diminished libido and infertility.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA

items.subtopic: PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Hyperprolactinemia
items.content: The diagnosis of hyperprolactinemia involves excluding known causes and confirming with a normal pituitary MRI. Idiopathic hyperprolactinemia is diagnosed when no other cause is identified, although small microadenomas may not be visible on MRI.
keyPoints: Exclude known causes such as drugs, hypothyroidism, renal failure, and physiological states.
Normal pituitary MRI supports the diagnosis of idiopathic hyperprolactinemia.
Small microadenomas (<2 mm) might evade detection by standard MRI.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA

items.subtopic: PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA
items.title: Elevated PRL Levels and Associated Conditions
items.content: Hyperprolactinemia can result from hypothalamic-pituitary lesions, plurihormonal adenomas, or drug effects. Elevated PRL levels typically range from 30–100 µg/L due to these causes. Dopaminergic disruption is a common mechanism.
keyPoints: Elevated PRL (30-100 µg/L) can result from hypothalamic-pituitary lesions, plurihormonal adenomas, or drugs.
Dopaminergic receptor inhibition by drugs like antipsychotics and antidepressants is a frequent cause.
Conditions include hypothalamic tumors, cysts, infiltrative disorders, and radiation-induced damage.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PITUITARY ADENOMAS AND HYPERSECRETION SYNDROMES (part 2) | HYPERPROLACTINEMIA

items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Galactorrhea
items.content: Galactorrhea is defined as the inappropriate discharge of milk-containing fluid from the breast, persisting longer than 6 months postpartum or after breastfeeding cessation. It can be unilateral or bilateral, with varying colors and consistency. Bloody discharges warrant imaging such as mammography or ultrasound to rule out malignancy. Hyperprolactinemia is a common cause, necessitating evaluation of PRL levels.
keyPoints: Galactorrhea persists longer than 6 months postpartum or after breastfeeding cessation.
Bloody discharges require imaging (mammography/ultrasound) to rule out breast cancer.
Normal basal fasting morning PRL levels are <20 µg/L; markedly elevated levels (>1000 µg/L) may need sample dilution for accurate measurement.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · GALACTORRRHEA | Hyperprolactinemia

items.title: Management of Hyperprolactinemia and Its Etiologies
items.content: Treatment of hyperprolactinemia aims to normalize PRL levels, alleviating symptoms like galactorrhea. Dopamine agonists are effective for most causes. If medication-induced, withdrawal is preferred; otherwise, dose titration or dopamine agonist addition may be necessary, especially in psychiatric patients. Hyperprolactinemia can resolve with thyroid hormone replacement in hypothyroidism or after renal transplantation.
keyPoints: Treatment aims to normalize PRL levels to alleviate symptoms and preserve bone density.
Dopamine agonists are effective for most causes of hyperprolactinemia.
Hyperprolactinemia resolves with thyroid hormone replacement in hypothyroid patients or after renal transplantation.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · GALACTORRRHEA | Hyperprolactinemia

items.title: Etiology and Prevalence of Prolactinomas
items.content: Prolactinomas are the most common type of functioning pituitary tumors, arising from lactotropes. They have a population prevalence of approximately 10/100,000 in men and 30/100,000 in women. Microadenomas (<1 cm) are more prevalent in women with a female-to-male ratio of 20:1, while macroadenomas (≥1 cm), which may be locally invasive, have a near equal sex distribution.
keyPoints: Prolactinomas account for about half of all functioning pituitary tumors.
Population prevalence is ~10/100,000 in men and ~30/100,000 in women.
Microadenomas are <1 cm; macroadenomas are ≥1 cm.
Female-to-male ratio for microprolactinomas is 20:1; sex ratio near 1:1 for macroadenomas.
Tumor size correlates with PRL concentrations; values >250 µg/L usually indicate macroadenomas.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PROLACTINOMA

items.title: Diagnosis and Presentation of Prolactinoma
items.content: Prolactinomas typically present differently in men and women due to hormonal effects. Women often experience amenorrhea, infertility, and galactorrhea, while men may have impotence, loss of libido, or CNS compression symptoms like headaches and visual defects. A prolactin level >200 µg/L suggests a prolactinoma if other causes are excluded. Levels <100 µg/L can still be due to microadenomas or nonneoplastic causes. An MRI is recommended for all patients with hyperprolactinemia, as PRL suppression by dopamine agonists does not confirm the lesion as a prolactinoma.
keyPoints: Women present with amenorrhea, infertility, and galactorrhea; men with impotence or CNS symptoms.
PRL level >200 µg/L suggests prolactinoma if other causes are excluded.
PRL levels <100 µg/L may indicate microadenomas or nonneoplastic causes.
MRI is recommended for all patients with hyperprolactinemia.
PRL suppression by dopamine agonists does not confirm a prolactinoma.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PROLACTINOMA

items.title: Management and Monitoring of Prolactinomas Based on Size and Symptoms
items.content: Asymptomatic microadenomas may not require treatment if fertility is not desired; these patients should be monitored with regular serial prolactin (PRL) measurements and MRI scans. Symptomatic microadenomas are treated to control hyperprolactinemia, reduce tumor size, restore menses and fertility, and resolve galactorrhea. Dopamine agonists like cabergoline or bromocriptine are used for both micro- and macroprolactinomas. For macroadenomas, formal visual field testing is recommended before starting dopamine agonists.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic microadenomas may not need treatment if fertility is not desired; monitor with serial PRL measurements and MRI scans.
Symptomatic microadenomas treated to control hyperprolactinemia, reduce tumor size, restore menses/fertility, resolve galactorrhea.
Dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) are mainstay for micro- or macroprolactinomas.
Formal visual field testing recommended before initiating dopamine agonists in macroadenoma cases.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Prolactinoma

items.title: Efficacy and Monitoring of Cabergoline Therapy in Prolactinomas
items.content: Cabergoline, a long-acting dopamine agonist with high D₂ receptor affinity, effectively suppresses prolactin (PRL) for >14 days after a single dose. It achieves normoprolactinemia and normal gonadal function in ~80% of microadenoma patients; galactorrhea improves or resolves in 90%. Cabergoline shrinks ~70% of macroprolactinomas, improving mass effect symptoms like headaches and visual disorders within days. MRI should be repeated within 16 weeks after initial therapy for macroadenomas. After achieving PRL control, cabergoline dose should be reduced to the lowest effective maintenance level.
keyPoints: Cabergoline suppresses PRL >14 days post single dose; high D₂ receptor affinity.
~80% of microadenoma patients achieve normoprolactinemia and normal gonadal function with cabergoline.
Galactorrhea improves/resolves in 90% of patients on cabergoline.
~70% of macroprolactinomas shrink with cabergoline; mass effect symptoms improve within days.
MRI should be repeated within 16 weeks after initial therapy for macroadenomas.
Reduce cabergoline to lowest effective maintenance dose post PRL control.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Prolactinoma

items.title: Dopamine Agonist Side Effects and Management
items.content: Dopamine agonists, used in treating pituitary tumors, can cause various side effects including constipation, nasal stuffiness, dry mouth, nightmares, insomnia, and vertigo. These are often dose-dependent and may be alleviated by reducing the dosage. Nausea, vomiting, and postural hypotension occur in approximately 25% of patients after the initial dose. Cabergoline is associated with fewer side effects compared to bromocriptine, which can be beneficial for the ~15% of patients intolerant to oral bromocriptine. Intravaginal administration of bromocriptine may help those with severe gastrointestinal issues.
keyPoints: Side effects include constipation, nasal stuffiness, dry mouth, nightmares, insomnia, vertigo
~25% experience nausea, vomiting, postural hypotension after initial dose
Cabergoline has fewer side effects than bromocriptine
~15% of patients intolerant to oral bromocriptine may tolerate cabergoline better
Intravaginal administration of bromocriptine can be effective for severe GI side effects
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · SIDE EFFECTS

items.title: Serious Side Effects and Monitoring in Dopamine Agonist Therapy
items.content: While dopamine agonists are generally well-tolerated, serious side effects such as auditory hallucinations, delusions, mood swings, impulse control disorders (up to 5% of patients), leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, pleural fibrosis, cardiac arrhythmias, and hepatitis have been reported. Patients with Parkinson's disease on ≥3 mg daily cabergoline may develop cardiac valve regurgitation. However, studies in prolactinoma patients receiving up to 2 mg weekly of cabergoline show no increased incidence of valvular disorders. Due to the lack of controlled prospective studies in pituitary tumor patients, it is prudent to perform echocardiograms before initiating standard-dose cabergoline therapy.
keyPoints: Serious side effects include auditory hallucinations, delusions, mood swings, impulse control disorders (up to 5%)
Rare reports of leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, pleural fibrosis, cardiac arrhythmias, hepatitis
Parkinson's patients on ≥3 mg daily cabergoline risk cardiac valve regurgitation
No increased valvular disorder incidence in prolactinoma patients on up to 2 mg weekly cabergoline
Echocardiograms recommended before starting standard-dose cabergoline therapy
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · SIDE EFFECTS

items.title: Management of Pituitary Tumors During Pregnancy
items.content: During pregnancy, the pituitary gland enlarges due to estrogen and other growth factors. This can lead to significant growth in a subset of macroadenomas (15–30%), necessitating careful monitoring and management strategies.
keyPoints: Pituitary enlargement during pregnancy is common due to hormonal influences.
5% of microadenomas significantly increase in size; 15-30% of macroadenomas grow during pregnancy.
Bromocriptine has been used for >30 years without teratogenic effects but should be discontinued upon confirmation of pregnancy.
For women with macroadenomas, regular visual field testing is recommended and bromocriptine may need to be reinstituted if tumor growth occurs.
Pituitary MRI during pregnancy is reserved for symptomatic patients with severe headache or visual defects.
Surgical decompression might be necessary if vision is threatened.
Cabergoline is not recommended when fertility is desired due to its long-acting nature and high $ D_{2} $-receptor affinity.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PREGNANCY

items.title: Bromocriptine Use in Pregnant Women with Hyperprolactinemia
items.content: For women on bromocriptine who wish to conceive, mechanical contraception should be used for three menstrual cycles before attempting pregnancy. Upon confirmation of pregnancy, discontinue bromocriptine and monitor PRL levels closely.
keyPoints: Bromocriptine has been safely used for over 30 years in women with hyperprolactinemia without teratogenic effects.
Mechanical contraception is advised through three menstrual cycles before conception when on bromocriptine.
Discontinue bromocriptine upon pregnancy confirmation and monitor PRL levels, especially if headaches or visual symptoms occur.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · PREGNANCY

items.title: Etiology and Ectopic Sources of GH Hypersecretion
items.content: Acromegaly is primarily caused by somatotrope adenomas, but can also result from mixed mammosomatotrope tumors, acidophilic stem cell adenomas, or ectopic sources such as pancreatic, ovarian, lung, or hematopoietic tumors. Excess GHRH production, often due to carcinoid tumors, is another rare cause.
keyPoints: GH hypersecretion usually results from somatotrope adenoma.
Mixed mammosomatotrope and acidophilic stem cell adenomas can also secrete GH and PRL.
Ectopic sources include pancreatic, ovarian, lung, or hematopoietic tumors.
GHRH-mediated acromegaly is often due to carcinoid tumors.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · ACROMEGALY

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Acromegaly
items.content: Acromegaly presents with indolent symptoms that may take over a decade for diagnosis. Key features include acral bony overgrowth, soft tissue swelling, cardiovascular complications, sleep apnea, diabetes mellitus, and increased risk of colon polyps.
keyPoints: Symptoms often remain undiagnosed for 10+ years.
Acral bony overgrowth includes frontal bossing and mandibular enlargement.
Cardiovascular impact includes cardiomyopathy and hypertension.
>60% have sleep apnea; 25% develop diabetes mellitus.
Increased risk of colon polyps, with up to one-third diagnosed.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · ACROMEGALY

items.title: Laboratory Investigation for Acromegaly Diagnosis
items.content: Diagnosis is confirmed by failure of GH suppression to <0.4 μg/L after an oral glucose load, with normal nadir levels being even lower (<0.05 μg/L) using ultrasensitive assays. IGF-1 levels are elevated in acromegaly and serve as a useful screening measure.
keyPoints: IGF-1 levels are elevated in acromegaly.
GH suppression test: failure to suppress GH <0.4 μg/L after 75 g glucose load confirms diagnosis.
Normal nadir GH levels with ultrasensitive assays are <0.05 μg/L.
20% of patients may show paradoxical GH rise post-glucose.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · ACROMEGALY

items.title: Why Transsphenoidal Surgery is Preferred for Acromegaly Treatment
items.content: Transsphenoidal surgery is the preferred primary treatment for acromegaly due to its high remission rates and rapid normalization of GH levels. It effectively reduces tumor size, with immediate improvements in soft tissue swelling post-surgery.
keyPoints: Preferred primary treatment for both microadenomas (~70% remission) and macroadenomas (<50% remission).
GH levels normalize within an hour; IGF-1 levels normalize within 3–4 days after surgery.
Acromegaly may recur in ~10% of patients; hypopituitarism develops in up to 15% post-surgery.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Acromegaly | SURGERY | SOMATOSTATIN RECEPTOR LIGANDS

items.title: How Somatostatin Receptor Ligands (SRLs) Manage Acromegaly
items.content: SRLs, including octreotide and lanreotide, are effective in normalizing GH and IGF-1 levels in ~50% of patients. They provide rapid symptomatic relief and can reduce pituitary tumor size.
keyPoints: GH and IGF-1 levels normalized in ~50% of patients using SRLs.
Octreotide LAR provides up to 6 weeks of GH suppression after a single injection.
Lanreotide controls GH hypersecretion in about two-thirds of treated patients with every 4-6 week administration.
Pasireotide is effective for patients resistant to octreotide or lanreotide.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Acromegaly | SURGERY | SOMATOSTATIN RECEPTOR LIGANDS

items.title: Side Effects of Somatostatin Receptor Ligands in Acromegaly Treatment
items.content: While SRLs are generally well-tolerated, they can cause gastrointestinal side effects and glucose intolerance. Pasireotide has a higher prevalence of glucose intolerance.
keyPoints: Common side effects include nausea, abdominal discomfort, fat malabsorption, diarrhea, and flatulence in one-third of patients.
Up to 30% develop long-term echogenic sludge or cholesterol gallstones.
Mild glucose intolerance due to transient insulin suppression is common; Pasireotide has a higher prevalence of new-onset diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Acromegaly | SURGERY | SOMATOSTATIN RECEPTOR LIGANDS

items.subtopic: GH RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST | DOPAMINE AGONISTS | RADIATION THERAPY | SUMMARY
items.title: Pegvisomant in GH Hypersecretion: Mechanism and Monitoring
items.content: Pegvisomant is used to antagonize endogenous GH by blocking its receptor, thereby normalizing IGF-1 levels in approximately 70% of patients. Despite this normalization, GH levels remain elevated as the drug does not affect the pituitary adenoma itself.
keyPoints: Normalizes IGF-1 in ~70% of patients
GH levels remain elevated
Administered by daily subcutaneous injection (10–30 mg)
Monitor tumor size with MRI due to potential side effects like liver enzyme elevation and lipodystrophy
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · GH RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST | DOPAMINE AGONISTS | RADIATION THERAPY | SUMMARY

items.subtopic: GH RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST | DOPAMINE AGONISTS | RADIATION THERAPY | SUMMARY
items.title: Radiation Therapy in Acromegaly: Efficacy and Side Effects
items.content: Radiation therapy is an adjuvant treatment for acromegaly, reducing tumor mass and attenuating GH levels over time. However, it requires a long duration to achieve significant GH suppression, with most patients needing 8-18 years for optimal results.
keyPoints: GH levels suppressed to <5 μg/L in ~50% of patients after at least 8 years
~90% of patients achieve this level after 18 years
Interim medical therapy often needed before maximal benefits are realized
Common side effects include hypothalamic-pituitary damage leading to hormone deficiencies within 10 years
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · GH RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST | DOPAMINE AGONISTS | RADIATION THERAPY | SUMMARY

items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of Cushing's Disease
items.content: Cushing's disease, caused by ACTH-producing pituitary adenomas, is distinguished from other causes of hypercortisolism through clinical features and dynamic testing. The unrestrained but partially suppressible ACTH secretion in these tumors allows for differentiation using high-dose glucocorticoid suppression tests.
keyPoints: ACTH-producing adenomas account for ~10–15% of all pituitary tumors.
Cushing's disease is 5–10 times more common in women than in men.
Dynamic testing with high-dose glucocorticoids helps distinguish pituitary from nonpituitary causes.
Mean basal ACTH levels are about eightfold higher in ectopic ACTH secretion compared to pituitary adenomas.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · CUSHING'S DISEASE (ACTH-PRODUCING ADENOMA)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Laboratory Investigation of Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: The diagnosis of Cushing's syndrome involves identifying pathologic cortisol excess and its etiology. Key features include central obesity, hypertension, and skin changes. Laboratory tests like 24-h UFC and dexamethasone suppression are used to confirm hypercortisolism.
keyPoints: Typical features: thin skin, central obesity, hypertension, moon facies, purple striae, glucose intolerance, osteoporosis.
Serum potassium <3.3 mmol/L in ~70% of ectopic ACTH secretion cases but <10% in pituitary-dependent Cushing's syndrome.
24-h UFC and overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test are used for diagnosis.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · CUSHING'S DISEASE (ACTH-PRODUCING ADENOMA)

items.title: Inferior Petrosal Venous Sampling in Cushing's Disease
items.content: When MRI is insufficient, inferior petrosal venous sampling helps localize ACTH-secreting pituitary adenomas. The procedure involves measuring the central:peripheral ACTH ratio before and after CRH administration to confirm a pituitary source.
keyPoints: MRI may not detect small (<2 mm) pituitary ACTH-secreting adenomas.
Central:peripheral ACTH ratio >2 before and >3 after CRH confirms pituitary adenoma.
Sensitivity of the test is >95%, with very rare false positives.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · CUSHING'S DISEASE (ACTH-PRODUCING ADENOMA)

items.title: Surgical Management and Outcomes for Cushing's Disease
items.content: Selective transsphenoidal resection is the primary treatment choice for Cushing's disease, with remission rates of approximately 80% for microadenomas. However, success drops below 50% for macroadenomas and is rare if adenoma visibility on MRI is absent.
keyPoints: Remission rate ~80% for microadenomas
<50% for macroadenomas
Surgery rarely successful without visible adenoma on MRI
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Cushing's Disease

items.title: Postoperative Management and Recurrence in Cushing's Disease
items.content: Following successful tumor resection, patients may experience ACTH deficiency requiring low-dose cortisol replacement. Biochemical recurrence occurs in about 5% of initially successful surgeries. Prophylactic thromboembolic management is recommended for vulnerable patients due to clotting defects from persistent hypercortisolemia.
keyPoints: ACTH deficiency may last up to 12 months post-surgery
Biochemical recurrence in ~5%
Prophylactic thromboembolic management advised
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Cushing's Disease

items.title: Pharmacological Options for Cushing's Disease When Surgery Fails
items.content: Pasireotide LAR can control hypercortisolemia in some patients with ACTH-secreting pituitary tumors, lowering plasma ACTH and normalizing UFC levels in ~20% of patients. Osilodrostat effectively normalized 24-h UFC in 86% of patients by inhibiting adrenal cortisol biosynthesis.
keyPoints: Pasireotide LAR lowers plasma ACTH in ~20%
Osilodrostat normalizes 24-h UFC in 86%
Hyperglycemia develops in up to 70% with Pasireotide
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Cushing's Disease

items.title: Diagnosis of Gonadotropin-Secreting Tumors Requires Immunohistochemistry
items.content: Gonadotropin-secreting pituitary adenomas often necessitate surgical resection for definitive diagnosis due to the limitations in distinguishing tumor-derived hormone levels from normal physiological variations using laboratory tests alone.
keyPoints: Immunohistochemical analysis of surgically resected tissue is crucial for diagnosing gonadotropin-secreting tumors.
Elevated free α subunit levels may be seen in 10–15% of patients with nonfunctioning tumors.
TRH stimulation test can induce an atypical increase in tumor-derived gonadotropins or subunits, aiding diagnosis.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · NONFUNCTIONING AND GONADOTROPIN-PRODUCING PITUITARY ADENOMAS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Pituitary Stalk Compression Effects
items.content: Nonfunctioning pituitary adenomas often present with symptoms related to mass effects, such as optic chiasm pressure. They can also cause hypogonadism due to compression of the pituitary stalk.
keyPoints: Most nonfunctioning adenomas are macroadenomas at diagnosis due to delayed symptom onset from mass effects.
Adenoma compression may lead to attenuated LH and features of hypogonadism.
PRL levels are usually slightly increased, but these tumors do not respond to dopamine agonists like prolactinomas.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · NONFUNCTIONING AND GONADOTROPIN-PRODUCING PITUITARY ADENOMAS

items.title: Laboratory Investigation for Hypopituitarism and Tumor Activity
items.content: Laboratory tests aim to classify tumor type, identify hormonal markers of activity, and detect hypopituitarism in patients with nonfunctioning pituitary adenomas.
keyPoints: Free α subunit levels may be elevated in 10–15% of patients.
Distinguishing between normal and tumor-derived FSH is challenging in peri- or postmenopausal women.
Increased gonadotropins alone are insufficient for diagnosing a gonadotropin-secreting tumor due to similarities with primary gonadal failure.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · NONFUNCTIONING AND GONADOTROPIN-PRODUCING PITUITARY ADENOMAS

items.subtopic: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas | TSH-SECRETING ADENOMAS
items.title: Management Strategy for Nonfunctioning Pituitary Microadenomas
items.content: Asymptomatic small nonfunctioning microadenomas with no threat to vision can be monitored with an MRI after 3 years, followed by serial MRI and visual field testing as needed. Macroadenomas require transsphenoidal surgery to reduce size and relieve compressive effects.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic small nonfunctioning microadenomas may be followed with MRI after 3 years.
Serial MRI and visual field testing are done thereafter as needed for microadenomas.
Transsphenoidal surgery is indicated for macroadenomas to reduce size and relieve compressive effects.
Vision improves in 70% of patients with preoperative visual field defects post-surgery.
MRI scans should be performed yearly starting ~6 months postoperatively to detect tumor regrowth.
~15% of nonfunctioning tumors recur within 5–6 years after successful surgical resection.
Adjuvant radiotherapy may be indicated if substantial tumor remains post-surgery.
Nonfunctioning pituitary tumors respond poorly to dopamine agonist treatment and SRLs are largely ineffective.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas | TSH-SECRETING ADENOMAS

items.subtopic: Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas | TSH-SECRETING ADENOMAS
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Management of TSH-Secreting Macroadenomas
items.content: TSH-producing macroadenomas are rare but often large and invasive, presenting with thyroid goiter and hyperthyroidism due to chronic overproduction of TSH.
keyPoints: TSH-producing macroadenomas are very rare.
They are often large and locally invasive when they occur.
Patients usually present with thyroid goiter and hyperthyroidism.
items.reference: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes · Nonfunctioning and Gonadotropin-Producing Pituitary Adenomas | TSH-SECRETING ADENOMAS

items.assertion: Craniopharyngiomas are often partially calcified and exhibit a characteristic appearance on skull x-ray and CT images.

items.assertion: Complete surgical resection of small pituitary adenomas usually cures hormone hypersecretion.
items.reason: Almost all pituitary adenomas are monoclonal in origin, implying the acquisition of one or more somatic mutations that confer a selective growth advantage.

items.assertion: MEN 1 is caused by inactivating germline mutations in MENIN.
items.reason: Loss of heterozygosity or a somatic mutation of the remaining normal MENIN allele leads to tumorigenesis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.reason: It has a 2-h serum half-life and possesses 40-fold greater potency than native somatostatin to suppress GH.

items.assertion: Surgery is the preferred primary treatment for GH-secreting microadenomas.
items.reason: The high frequency of residual GH hypersecretion after macroadenoma resection usually necessitates adjuvant or primary medical therapy.

items.assertion: Inferior petrosal venous sampling is used to distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors.
items.reason: Pituitary MRI with gadolinium enhancement is insufficiently sensitive for detecting small pituitary ACTH-secreting adenomas.
